nativeRange.habitat: Shallow margins of lakes, rivers, ponds, and wetlands; occasionally in slightly brackish coastal areas

decorPreferences: open planting area for background placement

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish or large cichlids

summary: Moneywort (Bacopa monnieri) is a beginner-friendly stem plant valued for its small, rounded, bright-green leaves and tolerant care requirements. It grows well as a mid- to background plant under moderate to high light.
careNotes: Moneywort is widely regarded as one of the more forgiving stem plants available to beginners, tolerating a broad range of water conditions and recovering well from neglect. Moderate to high lighting is recommended; stems that receive too little light tend to develop rot at the lower nodes. CO2 injection is not required, but providing it generally accelerates growth and improves the plant's overall density and color. A nutrient-rich substrate and regular liquid fertilization support healthy, sustained growth. Moneywort adapts to both submerged and emersed growth — when grown partially out of the water, it produces small pink-and-white flowers above the surface. The leaves are fleshy but delicate, so care during planting reduces damage. Stem cuttings root quickly and can be replanted directly into the substrate to propagate new plants or fill gaps in an existing planting. A 40-litre tank is a reasonable minimum for a small grouping of stems. A naturally shorter cultivar, sometimes sold as Bacopa monnieri 'Compact', typically reaches only 3–10 cm and tolerates lower light than the standard form.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward: trim healthy stem sections (5–10 cm) and plant directly into the substrate. The cut end quickly roots. Regular trimming and replanting maintains a dense, bushy appearance and prevents lower-stem die-back.

additional.name: Tropica
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing and water parameters. Note: Tropica item 4467 is the 'Compact' cultivar of B. monnieri, which is shorter (3–10 cm) and tolerates lower light than the standard species. Height and lighting for the standard species taken from primary source.
